disease.name: Cutaneous / Skin Abscess
disease.note: Localized collection of pus in the dermis or subcutaneous tissue. Painful, red, fluctuant swelling. Requires incision and drainage. Common in tropical settings where minor wounds become infected. In Kenya, abscesses are often seen after traditional medicine application, injections by unlicensed providers, or neglected minor trauma. The only absolute treatment is drainage — antibiotics alone will not resolve pus.
presentingComplaints: Swelling that has grown over days
Recent minor injury or injection at the site

history_questions.question: When did the swelling start?
Did it start from a small pimple or insect bite?
Have you had a fever?
Is the swelling getting bigger?
Is the area very painful?
Has any pus leaked out?
Did you have any recent injection at that site?
Did you put any traditional medicine on it?
Do you have diabetes?
Have you had abscesses before?
Are you on any medications that lower immunity?
Did you see a healthcare provider for it before?
Did you receive antibiotics for this?
Is there any history of trauma or injury at this site?
Do you inject any substances?
Have you had any surgery recently?
Have you had any previous abscesses? If so, how many and where?
Do you have diabetes, HIV, or any immune-weakening condition?
Have you been hospitalized or had surgery in the past 3 months?
Do you use intravenous drugs or have you had any recent injections?
Were you on antibiotics in the past month?
Have you had any trauma, insect bite, or injection at the site?
Do you have fever, chills, or night sweats?
Is the pain worsening or spreading beyond the swelling?
Have you applied any traditional or herbal remedies to the wound?
Do you have any drug allergies (especially to penicillin)?
Have you traveled recently or had contact with someone with a skin infection?
Do you have difficulty passing stool or rectal bleeding (for perianal abscess)?
Are you breastfeeding or pregnant (for breast abscess)?
Do you smoke cigarettes or use tobacco products?
Have you ever been told you have sickle cell trait or low hemoglobin?
Do you have any joint replacements or prosthetic devices?
Do you take steroids, chemotherapy, or immunosuppressants?
How rapidly has the swelling grown in the past few days?
Have you had similar abscesses in the same location before?
Do you have any numbness or tingling near the swelling?

examination_findings.finding: Fluctuant swelling on palpation
Erythema and warmth of overlying skin
Pointing pustule or necrotic center
Tenderness on palpation
Surrounding cellulitis
Regional lymphadenopathy
Fluctuance with well-defined margins
Spontaneous purulent discharge (if pointing)
Fever
Tachycardia (if systemic involvement)
Crepitus (rare, suggests gas-forming organism)
Limitation of movement if over joint
Induration around the abscess
Skin breakdown or impending rupture
Measure abscess dimensions (length x width x height in cm)
Bimanual palpation to detect fluctuance
Assess extent of surrounding erythema and induration
Palpate regional lymph nodes (epitrochlear, axillary, inguinal)
Inspect for lymphangitic streaks tracking proximally
Document body temperature
Check heart rate, blood pressure, and respiratory rate
Assess for skin necrosis, bullae, or hemorrhagic discoloration
Palpate for crepitus (subcutaneous gas)
Assess for pain out of proportion to physical findings
Check random blood glucose in diabetic patients
Assess range of motion of adjacent joints
Inspect skin for puncture wounds, injection sites, or foreign bodies
Assess sensation and motor function distal to the abscess
Perform digital rectal examination for perianal abscess
Assess for nipple discharge or peau d'orange for breast abscess
Inspect contralateral side for symmetry comparison
Assess for signs of dehydration (mucous membranes, skin turgor)
Document presence of previous surgical scars at or near the site
Assess for fluctuance after marking the margins of induration

bedside_scores.name: Abscess Severity Score (Clinical)
bedside_scores.interpretation: Score >=4 suggests need for GA drainage and IV antibiotics.

riskFactors.note: Common predisposing factor in Kenyan population

subtypes.name: Superficial Cutaneous Abscess
subtypes.management: I&D under LA. Pus culture. Wound packing. Remove packing in 24-48h. Antibiotics only if surrounding cellulitis or immunocompromised.

subtypes.name: Complex/Deep (e.g., Perianal, Pilonidal, Hand)
subtypes.management: I&D under GA if extensive. Loculi breakdown. Pus culture. Wound packing. IV antibiotics. Consider MRI/CT for deep extension.

subtypes.name: Perianal Abscess
subtypes.management: I&D via radial incision. EUA to identify internal opening. Fistulotomy if simple fistula. Antibiotics for cellulitis or immunocompromise. Stool softeners.

subtypes.name: Breast Abscess (Lactational)
subtypes.management: Ultrasound to confirm abscess vs mastitis. I&D or US-guided aspiration. Continue breastfeeding both sides. Anti-staphylococcal antibiotics. Do NOT stop breastfeeding.

subtypes.name: Injection Abscess
subtypes.management: I&D. Explore for retained needle. Culture-directed antibiotics. Tetanus prophylaxis. Counsel on safe injection practices.

subtypes.name: HIV-Associated Abscess
subtypes.management: I&D with biopsies for culture (routine, mycobacterial, fungal). Broader empiric antibiotics. Optimize ART. Lower threshold for admission. CD4 count. TB screening.

laboratory.name: HbA1c
laboratory.indication: screen for diabetes in recurrent abscess

management.protocolReference: IDSA Guidelines for Skin and Soft Tissue Abscess

resuscitation: Usually not required for superficial abscess
antibiotics: Not routinely needed for simple abscess after adequate drainage
Indications for antibiotics: surrounding cellulitis, immunocompromised, systemic sepsis, MRSA risk
Cephalexin 500 mg QDS or Clindamycin 300 mg QDS, TMP-SMX DS BD if MRSA risk

incision_and_drainage.description: Incision and drainage under LA (superficial) or GA (deep)
incision_and_drainage.step3: Make incision over most fluctuant area or at point of pointing
incision_and_drainage.step4: Break all loculi with a hemostat (gloved finger for large)
incision_and_drainage.step8: Pack wound loosely with ribbon gauze

general: Then daily dressing or let heal by secondary intention
If cavity large: repack for 2-3 days then remove
Keep wound clean and dry between dressing changes
discharge_criteria: Wound clean, no signs of systemic infection

pathways.treatment: Incision and drainage alone. No antibiotics. Pack wound.
I&D. Antibiotics covering Staph and Strep (Cephalexin, Clindamycin, TMP-SMX if MRSA risk). Wound culture.

operative_templates.procedure: Incision and Drainage of Cutaneous Abscess
operative_templates.incision: Linear or cruciate over most fluctuant area
steps: Prepare skin with antiseptic (povidone-iodine or chlorhexidine)
Break all loculi with finger or instrument

dischargeCriteria: Wound clean, packing removed or in place as planned
Follow-up appointment for wound check confirmed

patient_education.diagnosis_explanation: You have an abscess — a collection of pus (infected fluid) under your skin. The treatment requires opening the abscess to drain the pus. Antibiotics alone will not cure an abscess because the pus prevents them from reaching the bacteria.
patient_education.warningSigns: Fever, spreading redness, worsening pain, foul-smelling discharge, feeling generally unwell.
patient_education.activity: Normal activities as tolerated. Keep the wound area clean and dry.
patient_education.diet: Eat a healthy diet to support healing. Stay well hydrated.
patient_education.medication: Complete any prescribed antibiotics. Take pain medication as needed.
patient_education.followUp: Return for wound check in 24-48 hours. The packing will be removed or changed.

before_discharge: When to return for packing removal

after_discharge.action: Wound check — should be healing well

pathophysiologyDetail: A cutaneous abscess represents a localized collection of pus within the dermis and subcutaneous tissue, formed through a well-defined sequence of host-pathogen interaction. Bacterial inoculation occurs through a break in the skin barrier — often trivial (scratch, insect bite, injection site) — leading to neutrophil chemotaxis via IL-8 and complement cascade activation. As neutrophils phagocytose bacteria, they release proteolytic enzymes (matrix metalloproteinases, elastase) and reactive oxygen species that liquefy surrounding tissue, creating a cavity filled with a mixture of viable and degenerate neutrophils, bacteria, cellular debris, and fibrin. The host attempts to contain the infection by depositing a fibrin capsule (the 'pyogenic membrane'), which paradoxically prevents antibiotic penetration while also retarding bacterial spread. This is the fundamental reason why antibiotics alone are ineffective: they cannot reach therapeutic concentrations within the abscess cavity, and drainage is the only definitive treatment.

In the Kenyan context, cutaneous abscesses present with distinctive epidemiological patterns. Injection abscesses from unlicensed providers (common in rural areas where patients seek parenteral medications for symptomatic relief) account for up to 30% of surgical abscess consultations in some district hospitals. Traditional medicine application to wounds (herbal poultices, ashes, cow dung) introduces atypical and polymicrobial flora including Clostridium species and environmental gram-negatives. The microbiology accordingly differs from Western cohorts: Staphylococcus aureus remains predominant, but MRSA prevalence ranges from 15-35% across East Africa, and community-acquired MRSA (CA-MRSA), particularly the PVL-positive (Panton-Valentine leukocidin) strains, produces more aggressive infections with higher rates of necrotizing complications and recurrence. Streptococcus pyogenes, anaerobes (Bacteroides, Peptostreptococcus), and enteric gram-negatives (E. coli, Klebsiella) are common when the abscess is perianal, perineal, or associated with intravenous drug use.

landmarkTrials.trial: Antibiotics versus Incision and Drainage for Cutaneous Abscess (UK DURATION Trial)
landmarkTrials.population: 1,058 patients with uncomplicated cutaneous abscess across 48 UK emergency departments
landmarkTrials.intervention: I&D alone vs I&D + 7 days of oral antibiotics (co-amoxiclav or clindamycin)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Cure rate at 10 days: 82% I&D alone vs 86% I&D + antibiotics (p=0.12); recurrence at 30 days: 10% vs 7% (p=0.08)
landmarkTrials.impact: Demonstrated that antibiotics provide NO clinically significant benefit after adequate I&D for uncomplicated cutaneous abscesses
landmarkTrials.reference: Quinn JV, et al. Antibiotics versus placebo for the treatment of cutaneous abscess. BMJ. 2016;354:i3634.

landmarkTrials.trial: CA-MRSA Cutaneous Abscess Treatment (US Emergency ID NET)
landmarkTrials.population: 422 patients with cutaneous abscess in 11 US emergency departments
landmarkTrials.outcome: TMP-SMX did not improve cure rates (84% vs 83%, p=0.70) but reduced recurrence at 30 days (17% vs 28%, p=0.02)
landmarkTrials.impact: TMP-SMX after I&D reduces recurrence but does not improve initial cure; should be considered in high-recurrence populations like Kenya
landmarkTrials.reference: Miller LG, et al. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole versus placebo in cutaneous abscess. N Engl J Med. 2007;357(18):1785-1794.

landmarkTrials.trial: Loop Drainage vs Traditional I&D for Abscess
landmarkTrials.intervention: Loop drainage (Penrose drain through two small incisions) vs traditional I&D
landmarkTrials.outcome: Loop drainage reduced need for packing changes (1.0 vs 4.3, p<0.001), faster healing (7 vs 14 days), less pain
landmarkTrials.impact: Alternative technique that reduces wound care burden — valuable in resource-limited settings with limited nursing capacity
landmarkTrials.reference: Tsoraides SS, et al. Incision and loop drainage versus traditional incision and drainage for cutaneous abscess. J Pediatr Surg. 2017;52(4):641-644.

evidenceTable.outcome: Faster healing (7 vs 14 days), less pain

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: CONFIRM → LOCALIZE → DRAIN → FOLLOW. CONFIRM: The diagnosis of cutaneous abscess is clinical. Cardinal features: painful, erythematous, fluctuant swelling. The single most reliable physical sign is fluctuance — the sensation of fluid movement on bimanual palpation. If fluctuance is present, pus is present, and drainage is needed. If fluctuance is absent (indurated, non-fluctuant), the differential includes cellulitis (no pus, treat with antibiotics), infected sebaceous cyst, or tumor. Bedside ultrasound (if available) can differentiate abscess (hypoechoic/anechoic cavity with posterior enhancement and surrounding hyperemia) from cellulitis (diffuse tissue edema without a discrete fluid pocket). Ultrasound reduces the rate of 'dry taps' and unnecessary I&D attempts. LOCALIZE: Determine the full extent of the abscess cavity — mark the margins of induration and fluctuance. Identify whether the abscess is superficial (dermal/subcutaneous) or extends deeper into a potential space (perineal, perianal, axillary, subpectoral, psoas). Deep abscesses may require ultrasound or CT guidance for localization and drainage. Assess for surrounding cellulitis (measure and document the extent of erythema beyond the abscess margin). DRAIN: The definitive treatment is incision and drainage. For small abscesses (<2 cm) in easily accessible locations, local anesthesia (lidocaine 1% with epinephrine, field block) is adequate. For larger or deeper abscesses, procedural sedation or general anesthesia is preferable. Make an incision along skin crease lines (for cosmesis) over the point of maximal fluctuance, at least 1-2 cm long. A smaller incision is a common error — it prevents adequate drainage and forces re-presentation. Enter the cavity with a hemostat, break all loculi (digital or instrument), and evacuate all pus. Send pus for culture in selected cases (immunocompromised, recurrent, hospital-acquired, severe systemic toxicity). Irrigate with saline. Do NOT close the cavity — pack loosely with saline-moistened gauze or ribbon. For the loop drainage technique: make two small stab incisions at opposite poles of the abscess, pass a sterile Penrose drain or vessel loop through both openings, tie it in a loop, and leave it in situ for 7-10 days. Loop drainage requires less wound care and is ideal for busy outpatient settings. FOLLOW: The wound should be repacked daily (if packed) or the drain site checked (if loop). Pain typically improves within 24 hours. Antibiotics are NOT routinely indicated after adequate I&D for uncomplicated abscess in immunocompetent patients. Indications for antibiotics: extensive surrounding cellulitis (>2 cm), systemic toxicity/fever, immunocompromised host (diabetes, HIV, steroids), recurrent abscess, MRSA risk factors, or inadequate I&D (suspected residual collection). If indicated, choose an agent with MRSA activity: clindamycin 300 mg PO QDS or TMP-SMX 160/800 mg PO BD for 5-7 days.

managementPearls.pearl: The primary determinant of successful abscess treatment is ADEQUATE drainage, not antibiotics. A 1 cm incision in a 5 cm abscess is inadequate — it will close before the cavity collapses. The incision should be at least 50-75% of the diameter of the fluctuant area.

Pain during I&D is the most distressing aspect for patients. Adequate local anesthesia is achieved by: (1) buffering lidocaine with sodium bicarbonate (1:10 ratio) to reduce stinging, (2) injecting through the indurated skin, not into the cavity, (3) using a field block rather than a single depot, (4) waiting 5-7 minutes for full effect, and (5) using lidocaine with epinephrine (1:100,000) for hemostasis and prolonged effect.

In diabetic patients with cutaneous abscesses, blood glucose control MUST be addressed. Hyperglycemia (glucose >180 mg/dL) impairs neutrophil function and delays wound healing. Check HbA1c to identify undiagnosed diabetes — up to 20% of Kenyan patients presenting with abscess have previously undiagnosed diabetes.

The loop drainage technique (Penrose drain through two stab incisions, tied in a loop) is superior to traditional I&D with packing for abscesses >2 cm in cosmetically sensitive areas (axilla, groin, breast, face). It requires no daily packing changes, allows the patient to shower, and heals with a better cosmetic scar. It is ideal for Kenyan outpatient settings where daily wound care access is limited.

Recurrent abscesses in the same location should raise suspicion for: (1) inadequately drained cavity with retained foreign body, (2) pilonidal sinus (sacrococcygeal), (3) hidradenitis suppurativa (axillary/groin), (4) perianal fistula (perineal), or (5) osteomyelitis (if overlying bone). Do not simply re-drain — investigate the underlying cause.

operativeNuances.nuance: Incision placement is critical. For cosmetically sensitive areas, place the incision along a skin crease (Langer line) to minimize scar widening. For the face, a hidden incision (inside the nostril for nasal abscess, behind the hairline for scalp, within the oral mucosa for intraoral abscess) is preferred. For the breast, a periarolar or inframammary incision is more cosmetic than a direct incision over the abscess. For the perianal region, a radial incision (from the anal verge outward) preserves sphincter function and reduces the risk of fistula formation.

After draining the abscess, the cavity should be explored with a gloved finger (not just a hemostat) to break all loculi and ensure the cavity is a single, contiguous space. Multiple separate cavities require bridging incisions (counter-incisions) or the loop drainage technique. The wound should be packed loosely — tight packing causes pain, impedes drainage, and delays healing. Packing is used to keep the wound open (prevent premature skin closure), not to fill the cavity. A 1 cm ribbon gauze wick is sufficient for most wounds.

Cultures are not routinely needed but should be obtained in: HIV-positive patients (atypical mycobacteria, fungal), recurrent abscesses (MRSA, resistant organisms), immunocompromised hosts, severe systemic toxicity, and hospital-acquired infections. Wound cultures are more reliable than surface swabs. In Kenya, ESBL-producing Enterobacteriaceae are increasingly common in perineal and perianal abscesses; wound culture with sensitivity testing is essential for guiding therapy in these cases.

prognosis.healingTime: Resolution of pain within 24-48 hours; wound healing by secondary intention in 7-21 days depending on size
prognosis.recurrenceRate: 5-10% at 30 days; 15-20% at 1 year in immunocompetent patients; higher (30-50%) in diabetics and HIV-positive patients
prognosis.complicationRate: Cellulitis extension: 5%; bacteremia: 1-2%; scar hypertrophy: variable
prognosis.kenyanConsiderations: Recurrence and treatment failure are higher in Kenya due to higher MRLA prevalence, delayed presentation, and limited access to wound care
prognosis.keyDeterminants: Adequacy of initial drainage, host immune status, wound care access, and presence of underlying predisposing conditions (diabetes, HIV, hidradenitis)
